summarization.a: O K-edge/“O 1s” TFY-XAS for five films (a–e), each shown as-deposited (thin) and forming-gas annealed (FGA) (bold). The five samples are: (a) 117 nm non-doped, 400 °C, FeCp₂; (b) 328 nm, cycle ratio 100:1 (no Mg detected), 400 °C, FeCp₂; (c) 72 nm non-doped, 375 °C, CpFeC₅H₄CHN(CH₃)₂; (d) 425 nm, 100:2 (Mg/Fe = 0.017), 375 °C; (e) 411 nm, 100:10 (Mg/Fe = 0.062), 375 °C. Overall, the O-edge trends track the Fe-edge trends, with one noted exception: the 100:1 film looks hematite-like by Fe 2p but magnetite-like by O 1s.
summarization.b: Fe 2p (Fe L-edge) TFY-XAS for the same five films (a–e), again comparing as-deposited vs FGA. The 400 °C films (a,b) are described as hematite-like (more clearly split/double-peak L₃ structure), while the 375 °C films (c–e) are described as magnetite-like (lower-energy L₃ feature mainly a shoulder). FGA enhances/adjusts the lower-energy L₃ component (near ~708.5 eV) so the overall line shape becomes more magnetite-like.

a.question: What is being compared in each stacked sample trace in this TFY-XAS figure?
a.answer: For each sample (a–e), two spectra are shown: as-deposited (thin line) and forming-gas annealed (FGA) (bold line). This directly isolates the effect of the anneal on the O K-edge and Fe L-edge line shapes. The figure uses the same comparison across all five samples so temperature and Mg-cycling effects can be judged consistently.

a.question: Why is it useful to have both O-edge and Fe-edge XAS for the same samples when discussing magnetic performance?
a.answer: The magnetic behavior in this work is linked to whether the films are closer to hematite-like or magnetite-like chemistry. Showing both edges provides cross-checking: Fe 2p is sensitive to Fe oxidation/multiplet structure, while the O edge provides complementary information, and in most cases they trend together. The noted mismatch in the 100:1 sample (Fe-edge hematite-like, O-edge magnetite-like) flags that phase/chemistry assessment can be nontrivial from a single edge alone.

b.question: What is the key difference between the Fe 2p TFY-XAS of the 400 °C samples (a,b) and the 375 °C samples (c–e)?
b.answer: The 400 °C Fe 2p spectra (a,b) are described as hematite-like, with a more clearly split/double-peak L₃ structure. The 375 °C spectra (c–e) are described as magnetite-like, where the lower-energy L₃ feature is mainly a shoulder rather than a fully resolved peak. This separation is one of the main chemical-state indicators used in the paper for grouping samples by growth temperature.

b.question: Which Fe 2p spectral feature is used as evidence for Fe²⁺-containing (more reduced) iron oxide?
b.answer: A practical marker is an increased shoulder ~1–1.5 eV below the main L₃ (and similarly L₂) peak when Fe²⁺ is present. In this dataset, that shoulder is more prominent in the 375 °C group and becomes more emphasized after FGA, making the spectra resemble magnetite more closely. This is the specific line-shape argument cited in the discussion around Fig. 4.
